key_insight: Fluorination and correlated disorder can actively tune biphenylene’s anisotropic electronic transport, switching preferred conduction direction and enabling or suppressing negative differential resistance.
engineering_relevance: Controlled fluorination offers a practical route to engineer direction-dependent conductivity in 2D carbon devices and potentially modulate nanoscale switching behavior.
method: Density functional theory for electronic-structure analysis
Wannier-based tight-binding Hamiltonian for transport modeling
Quantum transport simulations under fluorination and disorder
evidence_signal: The abstract reports concentration-dependent anisotropic conduction, emergence of NDR and transport-direction inversion in ordered systems, and suppression toward nearly Ohmic behavior under disorder.
watch_item: The strongest transport effects depend on ordered or correlated fluorine patterns, so real-device variability and disorder could reduce reproducibility.
paper_title: Tunable Electronic and Transport Properties of Biphenylene via Fluorination and Disorder
